requiredExam.label: Inspect oral mucosa for hyperpigmentation

source.source_section: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) curated endocrine workup: required bedside maneuver
source.implementation_notes: Guideline-backed endocrine workup content curated into the local knowledge schema; schema, source, PHI, and regression tests run on 2026-06-06.

requiredExam.action: Use this maneuver to evaluate primary adrenal insufficiency, ACTH excess pattern, or congenital adrenal hyperplasia clue. Hyperpigmentation supports primary adrenal insufficiency or ACTH excess and changes ACTH, antibody, renin/aldosterone, or steroid-replacement interpretation.
requiredExam.rationale: Targets primary adrenal insufficiency, ACTH excess pattern, or congenital adrenal hyperplasia clue; positive, negative, or unable-to-assess findings guide escalation, diagnostic testing, treatment safety, or specialty planning for Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH).
requiredExam.technique: Inspect oral mucosa, palmar creases, scars, and sun-protected skin for diffuse or patchy hyperpigmentation.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: When primary adrenal insufficiency, Addison disease, ACTH excess, or congenital adrenal hyperplasia is possible
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: primary adrenal insufficiency, ACTH excess pattern, or congenital adrenal hyperplasia clue
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.
requiredExam.management_change: Hyperpigmentation supports primary adrenal insufficiency or ACTH excess and changes ACTH, antibody, renin/aldosterone, or steroid-replacement interpretation.
requiredExam.limitations: Inspect oral mucosa for hyperpigmentation is supportive for Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH), not diagnostic by itself; reliability can be reduced by pain, positioning, acute illness, cooperation, body habitus, baseline disability, and examiner technique.

reference_values: 17-OHP thresholds vary by age, gestational age, assay, cycle phase, and timing; morning follicular sampling improves adult NCCAH screening.
Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
decision_steps: For suspected classic CAH or crisis, check electrolytes, glucose, cortisol/ACTH context, 17-hydroxyprogesterone, renin/aldosterone, and treat instability immediately.
For nonclassic CAH, screen with early morning follicular 17-hydroxyprogesterone and confirm with cosyntropin-stimulated testing when borderline.
Interpret 17-OHP by age, gestational age, sex, assay, timing, menstrual phase, and glucocorticoid exposure.
Use androgen profile and CYP21A2 genetics when diagnosis, family planning, or genotype-phenotype counseling is needed.
Confirm that Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.

Order the first-line tests in sequence: 17-hydroxyprogesterone, often with cosyntropin stimulation; Electrolytes, glucose, renin/aldosterone; Androgens: testosterone, androstenedione, DHEAS as indicated.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: 17-OHP thresholds vary by age, gestational age, assay, cycle phase, and timing; morning follicular sampling improves adult NCCAH screening.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Treat adrenal crisis or salt-wasting with stress-dose hydrocortisone, fluids, dextrose, electrolyte correction, and mineralocorticoid/salt support as indicated.
Use physiologic glucocorticoid replacement for classic CAH and fludrocortisone when mineralocorticoid deficiency is present.
For nonclassic CAH, treat symptoms such as hirsutism, acne, menstrual dysfunction, or infertility rather than asymptomatic labs alone.
Monitor growth/puberty in younger patients and adult overtreatment risks including Cushingoid effects, bone, metabolic, fertility, and adrenal-rest tumors.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH); draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
For confirmed Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH), choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

provenance.source_material: Endocrine Society 2018 CAH guideline, GeneReviews 21-hydroxylase-deficient CAH chapter, DailyMed crinecerfont label, and local module evidence rows
provenance.review_note: CAH tree is threshold-cited and patient-traversable; TART ultrasound interval, prenatal dexamethasone research governance, elective genital surgery, assay-specific steroid interpretation, and local emergency/stress-dose order sets require clinician governance.

source_thresholds.label: Nonclassic CAH major-stress hydrocortisone only if cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal <14-18 ug/dL or iatrogenic suppression exists
Nonclassic CAH major-stress hydrocortisone only if cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal <14-18 ug/dL or iatrogenic suppression exists

exact_data_needed: Any neonatal ambiguous genitalia, salt-wasting episode, early pubarche, rapid growth, virilization, menstrual/fertility issue, or family CAH history?
How long has conception been attempted, are cycles ovulatory/regular, and have partner semen analysis or prior fertility evaluations found abnormalities?
Any personal or family autoimmune thyroid disease, type 1 diabetes, celiac disease, pernicious anemia, adrenal disease, pituitary disease, or polyglandular autoimmune syndrome?
Any vomiting, severe weakness, abdominal pain, fever, missed steroid doses, recent illness, or inability to keep down stress-dose steroids?
focused exam findings listed for this workup
weight when dosing depends on it
Hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe electrolyte/glucose abnormality, arrhythmia, airway compromise, or vision threat requires urgent escalation.
CYP21A2 genetic testing when diagnosis/family planning unclear.
Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions.

Adrenal insufficiency safety labs: check sodium, potassium, glucose, bicarbonate, creatinine, morning cortisol/ACTH or cosyntropin testing when stable, and renin/aldosterone or 17-OHP context for mineralocorticoid/CAH decisions; do not delay stress-dose steroids in shock.
cosyntropin plan before steroid treatment when feasible
imaging, ECG, culture, pathology, or procedure result required by the chosen branch
Compare with prior results and repeat discordant nonurgent endocrine tests using correct timing, local assay, and interference precautions.

Adrenal insufficiency safety labs: check sodium, potassium, glucose, bicarbonate, creatinine, morning cortisol/ACTH or cosyntropin testing when stable, and renin/aldosterone or 17-OHP context for mineralocorticoid/CAH decisions; do not delay stress-dose steroids in shock.
Morning cortisol/ACTH or cosyntropin for adrenal insufficiency; aldosterone-renin ratio with potassium correction for primary aldosteronism; metanephrines for PPGL.

Cushing screening with 1-mg DST, late-night salivary cortisol, or 24-hour UFC after excluding exogenous steroids; adrenal imaging follows biochemical framing except emergency/known incidentaloma context.
pregnant/not pregnant/unknown when pregnancy potential exists
postpartum/lactation status if testing or treatment could change
fertility intent when treatment depends on it
17-OHP thresholds vary by age, gestational age, assay, cycle phase, and timing; morning follicular sampling improves adult NCCAH screening.

Important mimics/exclusions: exogenous glucocorticoid exposure or withdrawal, primary versus central adrenal insufficiency, adrenal incidentaloma, medication-altered renin/aldosterone testing, catecholamine excess, severe illness, infection, and electrolyte mimics.
Electrolyte crisis or vomiting changes to emergency steroid/fluid pathway.
Unstable physiology, endocrine crisis, pregnancy-critical medication issue, or dangerous electrolyte/glucose abnormality changes disposition to urgent monitored care.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.
Suspected adrenal crisis is treated immediately with stress-dose steroids and fluids; testing must not delay treatment when unstable.

root.description: Activate for known or suspected 21-hydroxylase-deficient CAH, positive newborn screen, ambiguous genitalia/virilization, salt-wasting symptoms, adrenal crisis risk, early pubarche or hyperandrogenism, infertility/miscarriage context, chronic CAH medication review, crinecerfont review, pregnancy planning, or clinician-opened CAH evaluation.

endpoint_missing_context.description: Use this branch when the chart does not contain enough CAH-specific data to distinguish salt-wasting emergency, diagnostic confirmation, classic treatment, nonclassic treatment, pregnancy/DSD review, or routine follow-up.
missing_any: gestational age or birth weight when newborn
pregnancy or fertility intent when relevant

congenital_adrenal_hyperplasia_initial_a.description: Initial CAH assessment captures the specific patient data for emergency treatment, diagnosis, chronic replacement, nonclassic management, medication safety, and follow-up.

criteria_options.label: Newborn CAH screen: interpret 17-OHP by gestational age and confirm with LC-MS/MS or cosyntropin profile when first-tier results are positive or borderline
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends 17-hydroxyprogesterone newborn screening norms stratified by gestational age, second-tier LC-MS/MS when available, and cosyntropin stimulation testing before corticosteroids when immunoassay resources cannot improve specificity.
data_needed: cosyntropin plan before steroid treatment when feasible
criteria_options.source_section: Newborn screening recommendations 1.2-1.3 and diagnosis recommendation 3.1

criteria_options.label: CAH cosyntropin profile: 0.25 mg IV cosyntropin, 0.125 mg in very-low-birth-weight infants, baseline and 60-minute steroids after the first 24-48 hours of life
criteria_options.criteria_text: The Endocrine Society diagnostic profile uses 0.25 mg IV cosyntropin, reduced to 0.125 mg in very-low-birth-weight infants, with baseline and 60-minute samples; testing should be deferred until after the first 24 to 48 hours of life because immediate postbirth sampling has false positives and false negatives.
criteria_options.source_section: Diagnosis evidence and cosyntropin testing details

criteria_options.label: After infancy 17-OHP cutoffs: classic CAH >10,000 ng/dL; nonclassic baseline 200-10,000 ng/dL and ACTH-stimulated 1,000-10,000 ng/dL
criteria_options.criteria_text: GeneReviews tabulates diagnostic 17-OHP ranges after infancy: classic CAH baseline or ACTH-stimulated 17-OHP greater than 10,000 ng/dL (300 nmol/L); nonclassic CAH baseline 200-10,000 ng/dL (6-300 nmol/L) or ACTH-stimulated 1,000-10,000 ng/dL (31-300 nmol/L); unaffected baseline is below 200 ng/dL and stimulated below 1,000 ng/dL.
criteria_options.source_section: GeneReviews diagnostic testing table; Endocrine Society diagnosis recommendations 3.2-3.4

criteria_options.label: Salt-wasting CAH emergency: infant in first 4 weeks with dehydration, vomiting, shock, low sodium/chloride/CO2, high potassium, hypoglycemia, or inappropriately high urine sodium
criteria_options.criteria_text: GeneReviews identifies any infant with salt-losing crisis in the first 4 weeks of life as a CAH suspect; untreated or poorly controlled salt wasting may show decreased sodium, chloride, and total CO2, increased potassium, and inappropriately high urine sodium.
criteria_options.source_section: GeneReviews suggestive findings and clinical features; Endocrine Society stress/adrenal crisis recommendations

criteria_options.label: CAH stress-dose trigger: febrile illness temperature >38.5 C, dehydration gastroenteritis, major surgery with anesthesia, or major trauma
criteria_options.criteria_text: Increase glucocorticoid dosing in CAH patients who require glucocorticoids for febrile illness >38.5 C, gastroenteritis with dehydration, major surgery with general anesthesia, or major trauma; do not increase for everyday emotional stress or minor illness.
criteria_options.source_section: Stress dosing recommendations 4.7-4.11

criteria_options.label: CAH adrenal crisis injection: hydrocortisone 25 mg infants/preschool, 50 mg school-age children, 100 mg adults; then one-quarter dose every 6 hours
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggested initial parenteral hydrocortisone doses for adrenal crisis are 25 mg for infants and preschool children, 50 mg for school-age children, and 100 mg for adults; successive IV hydrocortisone may be one-quarter of the initial dose every 6 hours.
data_needed: weight if local protocol is weight-based
criteria_options.source_section: Stress dosing Table 4 and adrenal crisis evidence

criteria_options.label: Growing classic CAH maintenance: hydrocortisone 10-15 mg/m2/day three times daily, fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day, and infancy sodium chloride 1-2 g/day
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society Table 2 lists maintenance therapy in growing classic CAH as hydrocortisone tablets 10-15 mg/m2/day divided three times daily, fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day in 1-2 doses, and sodium chloride 1-2 g/day (17-34 mEq/day) in infancy divided into feedings.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment of classic CAH recommendations 4.1-4.6 and Table 2

criteria_options.label: Adult classic CAH maintenance: hydrocortisone 15-25 mg/day or selected long-acting glucocorticoid plus fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society Table 3 suggests adult classic CAH maintenance options: hydrocortisone 15-25 mg/day in 2-3 doses, prednisone 5-7.5 mg/day in 2 doses, prednisolone 4-6 mg/day in 2 doses, methylprednisolone 4-6 mg/day in 2 doses, dexamethasone 0.25-0.5 mg/day once daily, and fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day in 1-2 doses.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment of classic CAH recommendations 4.4-4.6 and Table 3

criteria_options.label: Nonclassic CAH major-stress hydrocortisone only if cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal <14-18 ug/dL (<400-500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic suppression exists
criteria_options.criteria_text: For nonclassic CAH, hydrocortisone stress dosing for major surgery, trauma, or childbirth is suggested only when cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal (<14 to 18 ug/dL or <400 to 500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic adrenal suppression is present.
criteria_options.source_section: Nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia recommendation 5.6

criteria_options.label: Nonclassic CAH daily steroid rules: treat rapid pubarche, advanced bone age, overt virilization, infertility, or patient-important hyperandrogenism; do not treat asymptomatic nonpregnant patients
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggests glucocorticoids for children/adolescents with inappropriately early rapid pubarche or bone age advancement, adolescents with overt virilization, and adult women with patient-important hyperandrogenism or infertility; it recommends against glucocorticoids in asymptomatic nonpregnant nonclassic CAH and suggests discontinuation when adult height is attained or symptoms resolve.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment of nonclassic CAH recommendations 5.1-5.5

criteria_options.label: CAH pediatric monitoring: age <=18 months monitor first 3 months of life and every 3 months thereafter; age >18 months evaluate every 4 months
criteria_options.criteria_text: For CAH patients <=18 months, monitor closely in the first 3 months of life and every 3 months thereafter; after 18 months, evaluate every 4 months, with pediatric growth, weight, blood pressure, exam, and biochemical measurements.
criteria_options.source_section: Monitoring therapy recommendations 4.12-4.16

criteria_options.label: CAH monitoring targets: time hormones consistently; normal 17-OHP suggests overtreatment; follicular progesterone target <0.6 ng/mL when trying to conceive
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society advises consistently timed hormone measurements relative to medication and time of day, not fully suppressing endogenous adrenal steroids, and interpreting 17-OHP normalization as overtreatment; for women trying to conceive, follicular-phase progesterone target is below 0.6 ng/mL (2 nmol/L).
criteria_options.source_section: Monitoring therapy evidence and Table 5

criteria_options.label: CAH long-term surveillance: adults need annual exam; TART ultrasound interval differs by source and should use local endocrine policy
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends annual adult physical examination with blood pressure, BMI, Cushingoid features, and biochemical monitoring; it recommends periodic testicular ultrasound in males with classic CAH, with expert opinion suggesting about every 1-2 years in asymptomatic males. GeneReviews lists testicular adrenal rest tumor monitoring every 3-5 years after puberty. Because intervals differ, local endocrine policy should define the exact interval.
criteria_options.source_section: Long-term surveillance recommendation 6.13 and GeneReviews surveillance
criteria_options.reviewer_input_needed: Local endocrine policy must choose a TART ultrasound interval because Endocrine Society expert opinion and GeneReviews summarize different surveillance intervals.

criteria_options.label: CAH pregnancy/fertility rules: genetic counseling for pregnancy planning, continue hydrocortisone/prednisolone and fludrocortisone, avoid dexamethasone, stress-dose labor
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends genetic counseling for CAH patients/partners planning pregnancy, specialist endocrine management during pregnancy, continuing prepregnancy hydrocortisone or prednisolone and fludrocortisone with adjustment for insufficiency symptoms, considering dose increase in the second or third trimester, stress-dose glucocorticoids during labor and delivery, and avoiding placenta-crossing glucocorticoids such as dexamethasone in pregnancy or conception attempts.
criteria_options.source_section: Genetic counseling, fertility, and pregnancy recommendations 6.3-6.9

criteria_options.label: Prenatal dexamethasone for CAH risk: experimental only, IRB-approved protocol, fetal sex screening, outcomes capture
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society advises prenatal CAH therapy remains experimental, does not recommend specific protocols, and recommends prenatal therapy only through IRB-approved protocols at centers able to collect outcomes, with Y-chromosomal DNA screening in maternal blood to exclude male fetuses from potential treatment groups.
criteria_options.source_section: Prenatal treatment recommendations 2.1-2.3
criteria_options.reviewer_input_needed: Prenatal CAH dexamethasone requires formal research governance, maternal-fetal medicine/endocrinology review, and documented IRB-approved protocol.

criteria_options.label: Crinecerfont adjunct: classic CAH age >=4 years; adults 100 mg twice daily; pediatric doses 25/50/100 mg twice daily by 10-<20, 20-<55, or >=55 kg
criteria_options.criteria_text: DailyMed labeling indicates crinecerfont as adjunctive therapy to glucocorticoid replacement to control androgens in adults and pediatric patients 4 years and older with classic CAH. Adult dose is 100 mg orally twice daily with meals. Pediatric doses are 25 mg twice daily for 10 to less than 20 kg, 50 mg twice daily for 20 to less than 55 kg, and 100 mg twice daily for at least 55 kg. Continue glucocorticoid replacement, assess androstenedione beginning 4 weeks after initiation, and do not reduce glucocorticoid below replacement.
criteria_options.source_section: Indications and dosage sections 1-2

criteria_options.label: Crinecerfont safety: discontinue for clinically significant hypersensitivity, avoid severe renal impairment/ESRD, and double doses for CYP3A4 inducers per label
criteria_options.criteria_text: DailyMed labeling contraindicates crinecerfont in hypersensitivity to crinecerfont or excipients, directs discontinuation for clinically significant hypersensitivity, warns adrenal crisis can occur if glucocorticoids are inadequate, does not recommend use in severe renal impairment or end-stage renal disease, and modifies dosing with CYP3A4 inducers: strong inducers increase morning and evening dosage 2-fold; moderate inducers increase the evening dose 2-fold.
criteria_options.source_section: Contraindications, warnings, renal impairment, and drug interaction sections

criteria_options.label: Virilized 46,XX classic CAH: surgery is optional shared decision in experienced DSD center; consider delaying or observing
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society advises families of pediatric CAH patients, especially minimally virilized girls, be informed about options including delaying surgery or observation; surgery should be performed only in experienced centers with pediatric surgery/urology, endocrinology, anesthesiology, behavioral health, and social work, and decisions for minors remain a family shared decision with experienced consultants.
data_needed: informed consent and assent when applicable
criteria_options.source_section: Restoring functional anatomy by surgery recommendations 7.1-7.4
criteria_options.reviewer_input_needed: Elective genital surgery decisions require experienced DSD team review, family shared decision-making, and local legal/ethical governance.

congenital_adrenal_hyperplasia_missing_d.description: Use this branch when a patient has possible CAH but the exact diagnostic thresholds or exclusion data are unavailable.
missing_any: baseline and 60-minute cosyntropin steroid profile when borderline
CYP21A2 testing or family variant when diagnosis remains equivocal

congenital_adrenal_hyperplasia_adrenal_c.description: Use when salt-wasting crisis or adrenal crisis is suspected; treatment should not wait for full diagnostic confirmation in an unstable patient.

criteria_options.label: Newborn CAH screen: interpret 17-OHP by gestational age and confirm with LC-MS/MS or cosyntropin profile when first-tier results are positive or borderline
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends 17-hydroxyprogesterone newborn screening norms stratified by gestational age, second-tier LC-MS/MS when available, and cosyntropin stimulation testing before corticosteroids when immunoassay resources cannot improve specificity.
data_needed: cosyntropin plan before steroid treatment when feasible
criteria_options.source_section: Newborn screening recommendations 1.2-1.3 and diagnosis recommendation 3.1

criteria_options.label: Salt-wasting CAH emergency: infant in first 4 weeks with dehydration, vomiting, shock, low sodium/chloride/CO2, high potassium, hypoglycemia, or inappropriately high urine sodium
criteria_options.criteria_text: GeneReviews identifies any infant with salt-losing crisis in the first 4 weeks of life as a CAH suspect; untreated or poorly controlled salt wasting may show decreased sodium, chloride, and total CO2, increased potassium, and inappropriately high urine sodium.
criteria_options.source_section: GeneReviews suggestive findings and clinical features; Endocrine Society stress/adrenal crisis recommendations

criteria_options.label: CAH stress-dose trigger: febrile illness temperature >38.5 C, dehydration gastroenteritis, major surgery with anesthesia, or major trauma
criteria_options.criteria_text: Increase glucocorticoid dosing in CAH patients who require glucocorticoids for febrile illness >38.5 C, gastroenteritis with dehydration, major surgery with general anesthesia, or major trauma; do not increase for everyday emotional stress or minor illness.
criteria_options.source_section: Stress dosing recommendations 4.7-4.11

criteria_options.label: CAH adrenal crisis injection: hydrocortisone 25 mg infants/preschool, 50 mg school-age children, 100 mg adults; then one-quarter dose every 6 hours
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggested initial parenteral hydrocortisone doses for adrenal crisis are 25 mg for infants and preschool children, 50 mg for school-age children, and 100 mg for adults; successive IV hydrocortisone may be one-quarter of the initial dose every 6 hours.
data_needed: weight if local protocol is weight-based
criteria_options.source_section: Stress dosing Table 4 and adrenal crisis evidence

criteria_options.label: Nonclassic CAH major-stress hydrocortisone only if cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal <14-18 ug/dL (<400-500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic suppression exists
criteria_options.criteria_text: For nonclassic CAH, hydrocortisone stress dosing for major surgery, trauma, or childbirth is suggested only when cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal (<14 to 18 ug/dL or <400 to 500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic adrenal suppression is present.
criteria_options.source_section: Nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia recommendation 5.6

congenital_adrenal_hyperplasia_newborn_c.description: Use for stable newborns or infants with a positive CAH screen, virilization, family risk, or early salt-wasting concern who are not already in adrenal crisis.

criteria_options.label: Newborn CAH screen: interpret 17-OHP by gestational age and confirm with LC-MS/MS or cosyntropin profile when first-tier results are positive or borderline
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends 17-hydroxyprogesterone newborn screening norms stratified by gestational age, second-tier LC-MS/MS when available, and cosyntropin stimulation testing before corticosteroids when immunoassay resources cannot improve specificity.
data_needed: cosyntropin plan before steroid treatment when feasible
criteria_options.source_section: Newborn screening recommendations 1.2-1.3 and diagnosis recommendation 3.1

criteria_options.label: CAH cosyntropin profile: 0.25 mg IV cosyntropin, 0.125 mg in very-low-birth-weight infants, baseline and 60-minute steroids after the first 24-48 hours of life
criteria_options.criteria_text: The Endocrine Society diagnostic profile uses 0.25 mg IV cosyntropin, reduced to 0.125 mg in very-low-birth-weight infants, with baseline and 60-minute samples; testing should be deferred until after the first 24 to 48 hours of life because immediate postbirth sampling has false positives and false negatives.
criteria_options.source_section: Diagnosis evidence and cosyntropin testing details

criteria_options.label: Salt-wasting CAH emergency: infant in first 4 weeks with dehydration, vomiting, shock, low sodium/chloride/CO2, high potassium, hypoglycemia, or inappropriately high urine sodium
criteria_options.criteria_text: GeneReviews identifies any infant with salt-losing crisis in the first 4 weeks of life as a CAH suspect; untreated or poorly controlled salt wasting may show decreased sodium, chloride, and total CO2, increased potassium, and inappropriately high urine sodium.
criteria_options.source_section: GeneReviews suggestive findings and clinical features; Endocrine Society stress/adrenal crisis recommendations

congenital_adrenal_hyperplasia_after_inf.description: Use when possible CAH presents after infancy and early morning biochemical thresholds can separate classic, nonclassic, and mimic pathways.

criteria_options.label: CAH cosyntropin profile: 0.25 mg IV cosyntropin, 0.125 mg in very-low-birth-weight infants, baseline and 60-minute steroids after the first 24-48 hours of life
criteria_options.criteria_text: The Endocrine Society diagnostic profile uses 0.25 mg IV cosyntropin, reduced to 0.125 mg in very-low-birth-weight infants, with baseline and 60-minute samples; testing should be deferred until after the first 24 to 48 hours of life because immediate postbirth sampling has false positives and false negatives.
criteria_options.source_section: Diagnosis evidence and cosyntropin testing details

criteria_options.label: After infancy 17-OHP cutoffs: classic CAH >10,000 ng/dL; nonclassic baseline 200-10,000 ng/dL and ACTH-stimulated 1,000-10,000 ng/dL
criteria_options.criteria_text: GeneReviews tabulates diagnostic 17-OHP ranges after infancy: classic CAH baseline or ACTH-stimulated 17-OHP greater than 10,000 ng/dL (300 nmol/L); nonclassic CAH baseline 200-10,000 ng/dL (6-300 nmol/L) or ACTH-stimulated 1,000-10,000 ng/dL (31-300 nmol/L); unaffected baseline is below 200 ng/dL and stimulated below 1,000 ng/dL.
criteria_options.source_section: GeneReviews diagnostic testing table; Endocrine Society diagnosis recommendations 3.2-3.4

congenital_adrenal_hyperplasia_classic_r.description: Use for stable classic CAH patients who need maintenance glucocorticoid/mineralocorticoid replacement or dose review.

criteria_options.label: Growing classic CAH maintenance: hydrocortisone 10-15 mg/m2/day three times daily, fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day, and infancy sodium chloride 1-2 g/day
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society Table 2 lists maintenance therapy in growing classic CAH as hydrocortisone tablets 10-15 mg/m2/day divided three times daily, fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day in 1-2 doses, and sodium chloride 1-2 g/day (17-34 mEq/day) in infancy divided into feedings.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment of classic CAH recommendations 4.1-4.6 and Table 2

criteria_options.label: Adult classic CAH maintenance: hydrocortisone 15-25 mg/day or selected long-acting glucocorticoid plus fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society Table 3 suggests adult classic CAH maintenance options: hydrocortisone 15-25 mg/day in 2-3 doses, prednisone 5-7.5 mg/day in 2 doses, prednisolone 4-6 mg/day in 2 doses, methylprednisolone 4-6 mg/day in 2 doses, dexamethasone 0.25-0.5 mg/day once daily, and fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day in 1-2 doses.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment of classic CAH recommendations 4.4-4.6 and Table 3

criteria_options.label: CAH pediatric monitoring: age <=18 months monitor first 3 months of life and every 3 months thereafter; age >18 months evaluate every 4 months
criteria_options.criteria_text: For CAH patients <=18 months, monitor closely in the first 3 months of life and every 3 months thereafter; after 18 months, evaluate every 4 months, with pediatric growth, weight, blood pressure, exam, and biochemical measurements.
criteria_options.source_section: Monitoring therapy recommendations 4.12-4.16

criteria_options.label: CAH monitoring targets: time hormones consistently; normal 17-OHP suggests overtreatment; follicular progesterone target <0.6 ng/mL when trying to conceive
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society advises consistently timed hormone measurements relative to medication and time of day, not fully suppressing endogenous adrenal steroids, and interpreting 17-OHP normalization as overtreatment; for women trying to conceive, follicular-phase progesterone target is below 0.6 ng/mL (2 nmol/L).
criteria_options.source_section: Monitoring therapy evidence and Table 5

congenital_adrenal_hyperplasia_nonclassi.description: Use when nonclassic CAH is confirmed and symptoms or reproductive goals justify treatment.

criteria_options.label: Nonclassic CAH major-stress hydrocortisone only if cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal <14-18 ug/dL (<400-500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic suppression exists
criteria_options.criteria_text: For nonclassic CAH, hydrocortisone stress dosing for major surgery, trauma, or childbirth is suggested only when cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal (<14 to 18 ug/dL or <400 to 500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic adrenal suppression is present.
criteria_options.source_section: Nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia recommendation 5.6

criteria_options.label: Nonclassic CAH daily steroid rules: treat rapid pubarche, advanced bone age, overt virilization, infertility, or patient-important hyperandrogenism; do not treat asymptomatic nonpregnant patients
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggests glucocorticoids for children/adolescents with inappropriately early rapid pubarche or bone age advancement, adolescents with overt virilization, and adult women with patient-important hyperandrogenism or infertility; it recommends against glucocorticoids in asymptomatic nonpregnant nonclassic CAH and suggests discontinuation when adult height is attained or symptoms resolve.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment of nonclassic CAH recommendations 5.1-5.5

congenital_adrenal_hyperplasia_nonclassi.description: Use when nonclassic CAH does not justify daily glucocorticoids or when prior treatment can be tapered/stopped.

criteria_options.label: Nonclassic CAH major-stress hydrocortisone only if cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal <14-18 ug/dL (<400-500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic suppression exists
criteria_options.criteria_text: For nonclassic CAH, hydrocortisone stress dosing for major surgery, trauma, or childbirth is suggested only when cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal (<14 to 18 ug/dL or <400 to 500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic adrenal suppression is present.
criteria_options.source_section: Nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia recommendation 5.6

criteria_options.label: Nonclassic CAH daily steroid rules: treat rapid pubarche, advanced bone age, overt virilization, infertility, or patient-important hyperandrogenism; do not treat asymptomatic nonpregnant patients
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggests glucocorticoids for children/adolescents with inappropriately early rapid pubarche or bone age advancement, adolescents with overt virilization, and adult women with patient-important hyperandrogenism or infertility; it recommends against glucocorticoids in asymptomatic nonpregnant nonclassic CAH and suggests discontinuation when adult height is attained or symptoms resolve.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment of nonclassic CAH recommendations 5.1-5.5

congenital_adrenal_hyperplasia_crinecerf.description: Use when crinecerfont is being considered, started, monitored, dose-adjusted, or stopped.

criteria_options.label: Crinecerfont adjunct: classic CAH age >=4 years; adults 100 mg twice daily; pediatric doses 25/50/100 mg twice daily by 10-<20, 20-<55, or >=55 kg
criteria_options.criteria_text: DailyMed labeling indicates crinecerfont as adjunctive therapy to glucocorticoid replacement to control androgens in adults and pediatric patients 4 years and older with classic CAH. Adult dose is 100 mg orally twice daily with meals. Pediatric doses are 25 mg twice daily for 10 to less than 20 kg, 50 mg twice daily for 20 to less than 55 kg, and 100 mg twice daily for at least 55 kg. Continue glucocorticoid replacement, assess androstenedione beginning 4 weeks after initiation, and do not reduce glucocorticoid below replacement.
criteria_options.source_section: Indications and dosage sections 1-2

criteria_options.label: Crinecerfont safety: discontinue for clinically significant hypersensitivity, avoid severe renal impairment/ESRD, and double doses for CYP3A4 inducers per label
criteria_options.criteria_text: DailyMed labeling contraindicates crinecerfont in hypersensitivity to crinecerfont or excipients, directs discontinuation for clinically significant hypersensitivity, warns adrenal crisis can occur if glucocorticoids are inadequate, does not recommend use in severe renal impairment or end-stage renal disease, and modifies dosing with CYP3A4 inducers: strong inducers increase morning and evening dosage 2-fold; moderate inducers increase the evening dose 2-fold.
criteria_options.source_section: Contraindications, warnings, renal impairment, and drug interaction sections

congenital_adrenal_hyperplasia_pregnancy.description: Use when pregnancy, fertility, prenatal treatment, genetic counseling, or genital anatomy decisions alter CAH management.

criteria_options.label: CAH pregnancy/fertility rules: genetic counseling for pregnancy planning, continue hydrocortisone/prednisolone and fludrocortisone, avoid dexamethasone, stress-dose labor
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends genetic counseling for CAH patients/partners planning pregnancy, specialist endocrine management during pregnancy, continuing prepregnancy hydrocortisone or prednisolone and fludrocortisone with adjustment for insufficiency symptoms, considering dose increase in the second or third trimester, stress-dose glucocorticoids during labor and delivery, and avoiding placenta-crossing glucocorticoids such as dexamethasone in pregnancy or conception attempts.
criteria_options.source_section: Genetic counseling, fertility, and pregnancy recommendations 6.3-6.9

criteria_options.label: Prenatal dexamethasone for CAH risk: experimental only, IRB-approved protocol, fetal sex screening, outcomes capture
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society advises prenatal CAH therapy remains experimental, does not recommend specific protocols, and recommends prenatal therapy only through IRB-approved protocols at centers able to collect outcomes, with Y-chromosomal DNA screening in maternal blood to exclude male fetuses from potential treatment groups.
criteria_options.source_section: Prenatal treatment recommendations 2.1-2.3
criteria_options.reviewer_input_needed: Prenatal CAH dexamethasone requires formal research governance, maternal-fetal medicine/endocrinology review, and documented IRB-approved protocol.

criteria_options.label: Virilized 46,XX classic CAH: surgery is optional shared decision in experienced DSD center; consider delaying or observing
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society advises families of pediatric CAH patients, especially minimally virilized girls, be informed about options including delaying surgery or observation; surgery should be performed only in experienced centers with pediatric surgery/urology, endocrinology, anesthesiology, behavioral health, and social work, and decisions for minors remain a family shared decision with experienced consultants.
data_needed: informed consent and assent when applicable
criteria_options.source_section: Restoring functional anatomy by surgery recommendations 7.1-7.4
criteria_options.reviewer_input_needed: Elective genital surgery decisions require experienced DSD team review, family shared decision-making, and local legal/ethical governance.

congenital_adrenal_hyperplasia_monitorin.description: Use after any CAH diagnostic or treatment branch when response, dose toxicity, or long-term complication surveillance must be tracked.

criteria_options.label: CAH pediatric monitoring: age <=18 months monitor first 3 months of life and every 3 months thereafter; age >18 months evaluate every 4 months
criteria_options.criteria_text: For CAH patients <=18 months, monitor closely in the first 3 months of life and every 3 months thereafter; after 18 months, evaluate every 4 months, with pediatric growth, weight, blood pressure, exam, and biochemical measurements.
criteria_options.source_section: Monitoring therapy recommendations 4.12-4.16

criteria_options.label: CAH monitoring targets: time hormones consistently; normal 17-OHP suggests overtreatment; follicular progesterone target <0.6 ng/mL when trying to conceive

criteria_options.source_section: Monitoring therapy evidence and Table 5

criteria_options.label: CAH long-term surveillance: adults need annual exam; TART ultrasound interval differs by source and should use local endocrine policy
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends annual adult physical examination with blood pressure, BMI, Cushingoid features, and biochemical monitoring; it recommends periodic testicular ultrasound in males with classic CAH, with expert opinion suggesting about every 1-2 years in asymptomatic males. GeneReviews lists testicular adrenal rest tumor monitoring every 3-5 years after puberty. Because intervals differ, local endocrine policy should define the exact interval.
criteria_options.source_section: Long-term surveillance recommendation 6.13 and GeneReviews surveillance
criteria_options.reviewer_input_needed: Local endocrine policy must choose a TART ultrasound interval because Endocrine Society expert opinion and GeneReviews summarize different surveillance intervals.

criteria_options.label: CAH adrenal crisis injection: hydrocortisone 25 mg infants/preschool, 50 mg school-age children, 100 mg adults; then one-quarter dose every 6 hours
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggested initial parenteral hydrocortisone doses for adrenal crisis are 25 mg for infants and preschool children, 50 mg for school-age children, and 100 mg for adults; successive IV hydrocortisone may be one-quarter of the initial dose every 6 hours.
data_needed: weight if local protocol is weight-based
criteria_options.source_section: Stress dosing Table 4 and adrenal crisis evidence

criteria_options.label: Nonclassic CAH major-stress hydrocortisone only if cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal <14-18 ug/dL (<400-500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic suppression exists
criteria_options.criteria_text: For nonclassic CAH, hydrocortisone stress dosing for major surgery, trauma, or childbirth is suggested only when cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal (<14 to 18 ug/dL or <400 to 500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic adrenal suppression is present.
criteria_options.source_section: Nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia recommendation 5.6

criteria_options.label: CAH pediatric monitoring: age <=18 months monitor first 3 months of life and every 3 months thereafter; age >18 months evaluate every 4 months
criteria_options.criteria_text: For CAH patients <=18 months, monitor closely in the first 3 months of life and every 3 months thereafter; after 18 months, evaluate every 4 months, with pediatric growth, weight, blood pressure, exam, and biochemical measurements.
criteria_options.source_section: Monitoring therapy recommendations 4.12-4.16

criteria_options.label: CAH monitoring targets: time hormones consistently; normal 17-OHP suggests overtreatment; follicular progesterone target <0.6 ng/mL when trying to conceive
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society advises consistently timed hormone measurements relative to medication and time of day, not fully suppressing endogenous adrenal steroids, and interpreting 17-OHP normalization as overtreatment; for women trying to conceive, follicular-phase progesterone target is below 0.6 ng/mL (2 nmol/L).
criteria_options.source_section: Monitoring therapy evidence and Table 5

criteria_options.label: Crinecerfont adjunct: classic CAH age >=4 years; adults 100 mg twice daily; pediatric doses 25/50/100 mg twice daily by 10-<20, 20-<55, or >=55 kg
criteria_options.criteria_text: DailyMed labeling indicates crinecerfont as adjunctive therapy to glucocorticoid replacement to control androgens in adults and pediatric patients 4 years and older with classic CAH. Adult dose is 100 mg orally twice daily with meals. Pediatric doses are 25 mg twice daily for 10 to less than 20 kg, 50 mg twice daily for 20 to less than 55 kg, and 100 mg twice daily for at least 55 kg. Continue glucocorticoid replacement, assess androstenedione beginning 4 weeks after initiation, and do not reduce glucocorticoid below replacement.
criteria_options.source_section: Indications and dosage sections 1-2

criteria_options.label: Crinecerfont safety: discontinue for clinically significant hypersensitivity, avoid severe renal impairment/ESRD, and double doses for CYP3A4 inducers per label
criteria_options.criteria_text: DailyMed labeling contraindicates crinecerfont in hypersensitivity to crinecerfont or excipients, directs discontinuation for clinically significant hypersensitivity, warns adrenal crisis can occur if glucocorticoids are inadequate, does not recommend use in severe renal impairment or end-stage renal disease, and modifies dosing with CYP3A4 inducers: strong inducers increase morning and evening dosage 2-fold; moderate inducers increase the evening dose 2-fold.
criteria_options.source_section: Contraindications, warnings, renal impairment, and drug interaction sections

root.label: CAH pathway

criteria.description: Activate for known or suspected 21-hydroxylase-deficient CAH, positive newborn screen, ambiguous genitalia/virilization, salt-wasting symptoms, adrenal crisis risk, early pubarche or hyperandrogenism, infertility/miscarriage context, chronic CAH medication review, crinecerfont review, pregnancy planning, or clinician-opened CAH evaluation.

root.action: Route CAH through immediate crisis treatment when unstable; otherwise use exact age, gestational, 17-OHP, cosyntropin, electrolyte, phenotype, medication, pregnancy/fertility, and follow-up data to reach an actionable diagnostic, treatment, monitoring, review, or safety-net endpoint.

children.label: Internal traversal guard

criteria.description: Use this branch when the chart does not contain enough CAH-specific data to distinguish salt-wasting emergency, diagnostic confirmation, classic treatment, nonclassic treatment, pregnancy/DSD review, or routine follow-up.
missing_any: gestational age or birth weight when newborn
pregnancy or fertility intent when relevant

children.action: Internal structured traversal guard; hidden from the clinical pathway outline and graph.

display.reason: Internal traversal guard; exact unavailable fields remain in missing_data_needed.

missing_data_needed: gestational age and birth weight when newborn
newborn screen result or early morning 17-OHP with assay method
cosyntropin dose and 60-minute steroid profile when indicated

children.label: Initial CAH assessment
children.edgeLabel: CAH is already suspected or known: assess salt-wasting danger, virilization/hyperandrogenism phenotype, diagnostic thresholds, and current steroid/mineralocorticoid/crinecerfont safety

criteria.description: Initial CAH assessment captures the specific patient data for emergency treatment, diagnosis, chronic replacement, nonclassic management, medication safety, and follow-up.

criteria_options.label: Newborn CAH screen: interpret 17-OHP by gestational age and confirm with LC-MS/MS or cosyntropin profile when first-tier results are positive or borderline
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends 17-hydroxyprogesterone newborn screening norms stratified by gestational age, second-tier LC-MS/MS when available, and cosyntropin stimulation testing before corticosteroids when immunoassay resources cannot improve specificity.
data_needed: cosyntropin plan before steroid treatment when feasible
criteria_options.source_section: Newborn screening recommendations 1.2-1.3 and diagnosis recommendation 3.1

criteria_options.label: CAH cosyntropin profile: 0.25 mg IV cosyntropin, 0.125 mg in very-low-birth-weight infants, baseline and 60-minute steroids after the first 24-48 hours of life
criteria_options.criteria_text: The Endocrine Society diagnostic profile uses 0.25 mg IV cosyntropin, reduced to 0.125 mg in very-low-birth-weight infants, with baseline and 60-minute samples; testing should be deferred until after the first 24 to 48 hours of life because immediate postbirth sampling has false positives and false negatives.
criteria_options.source_section: Diagnosis evidence and cosyntropin testing details

criteria_options.label: After infancy 17-OHP cutoffs: classic CAH >10,000 ng/dL; nonclassic baseline 200-10,000 ng/dL and ACTH-stimulated 1,000-10,000 ng/dL
criteria_options.criteria_text: GeneReviews tabulates diagnostic 17-OHP ranges after infancy: classic CAH baseline or ACTH-stimulated 17-OHP greater than 10,000 ng/dL (300 nmol/L); nonclassic CAH baseline 200-10,000 ng/dL (6-300 nmol/L) or ACTH-stimulated 1,000-10,000 ng/dL (31-300 nmol/L); unaffected baseline is below 200 ng/dL and stimulated below 1,000 ng/dL.
criteria_options.source_section: GeneReviews diagnostic testing table; Endocrine Society diagnosis recommendations 3.2-3.4

criteria_options.label: Salt-wasting CAH emergency: infant in first 4 weeks with dehydration, vomiting, shock, low sodium/chloride/CO2, high potassium, hypoglycemia, or inappropriately high urine sodium
criteria_options.criteria_text: GeneReviews identifies any infant with salt-losing crisis in the first 4 weeks of life as a CAH suspect; untreated or poorly controlled salt wasting may show decreased sodium, chloride, and total CO2, increased potassium, and inappropriately high urine sodium.
criteria_options.source_section: GeneReviews suggestive findings and clinical features; Endocrine Society stress/adrenal crisis recommendations

criteria_options.label: CAH stress-dose trigger: febrile illness temperature >38.5 C, dehydration gastroenteritis, major surgery with anesthesia, or major trauma
criteria_options.criteria_text: Increase glucocorticoid dosing in CAH patients who require glucocorticoids for febrile illness >38.5 C, gastroenteritis with dehydration, major surgery with general anesthesia, or major trauma; do not increase for everyday emotional stress or minor illness.
criteria_options.source_section: Stress dosing recommendations 4.7-4.11

criteria_options.label: CAH adrenal crisis injection: hydrocortisone 25 mg infants/preschool, 50 mg school-age children, 100 mg adults; then one-quarter dose every 6 hours
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggested initial parenteral hydrocortisone doses for adrenal crisis are 25 mg for infants and preschool children, 50 mg for school-age children, and 100 mg for adults; successive IV hydrocortisone may be one-quarter of the initial dose every 6 hours.
data_needed: weight if local protocol is weight-based
criteria_options.source_section: Stress dosing Table 4 and adrenal crisis evidence

criteria_options.label: Growing classic CAH maintenance: hydrocortisone 10-15 mg/m2/day three times daily, fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day, and infancy sodium chloride 1-2 g/day
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society Table 2 lists maintenance therapy in growing classic CAH as hydrocortisone tablets 10-15 mg/m2/day divided three times daily, fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day in 1-2 doses, and sodium chloride 1-2 g/day (17-34 mEq/day) in infancy divided into feedings.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment of classic CAH recommendations 4.1-4.6 and Table 2

criteria_options.label: Adult classic CAH maintenance: hydrocortisone 15-25 mg/day or selected long-acting glucocorticoid plus fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society Table 3 suggests adult classic CAH maintenance options: hydrocortisone 15-25 mg/day in 2-3 doses, prednisone 5-7.5 mg/day in 2 doses, prednisolone 4-6 mg/day in 2 doses, methylprednisolone 4-6 mg/day in 2 doses, dexamethasone 0.25-0.5 mg/day once daily, and fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day in 1-2 doses.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment of classic CAH recommendations 4.4-4.6 and Table 3

criteria_options.label: Nonclassic CAH major-stress hydrocortisone only if cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal <14-18 ug/dL (<400-500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic suppression exists
criteria_options.criteria_text: For nonclassic CAH, hydrocortisone stress dosing for major surgery, trauma, or childbirth is suggested only when cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal (<14 to 18 ug/dL or <400 to 500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic adrenal suppression is present.
criteria_options.source_section: Nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia recommendation 5.6

criteria_options.label: Nonclassic CAH daily steroid rules: treat rapid pubarche, advanced bone age, overt virilization, infertility, or patient-important hyperandrogenism; do not treat asymptomatic nonpregnant patients
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggests glucocorticoids for children/adolescents with inappropriately early rapid pubarche or bone age advancement, adolescents with overt virilization, and adult women with patient-important hyperandrogenism or infertility; it recommends against glucocorticoids in asymptomatic nonpregnant nonclassic CAH and suggests discontinuation when adult height is attained or symptoms resolve.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment of nonclassic CAH recommendations 5.1-5.5

criteria_options.label: CAH pediatric monitoring: age <=18 months monitor first 3 months of life and every 3 months thereafter; age >18 months evaluate every 4 months
criteria_options.criteria_text: For CAH patients <=18 months, monitor closely in the first 3 months of life and every 3 months thereafter; after 18 months, evaluate every 4 months, with pediatric growth, weight, blood pressure, exam, and biochemical measurements.
criteria_options.source_section: Monitoring therapy recommendations 4.12-4.16

criteria_options.label: CAH monitoring targets: time hormones consistently; normal 17-OHP suggests overtreatment; follicular progesterone target <0.6 ng/mL when trying to conceive
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society advises consistently timed hormone measurements relative to medication and time of day, not fully suppressing endogenous adrenal steroids, and interpreting 17-OHP normalization as overtreatment; for women trying to conceive, follicular-phase progesterone target is below 0.6 ng/mL (2 nmol/L).
criteria_options.source_section: Monitoring therapy evidence and Table 5

criteria_options.label: CAH long-term surveillance: adults need annual exam; TART ultrasound interval differs by source and should use local endocrine policy
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends annual adult physical examination with blood pressure, BMI, Cushingoid features, and biochemical monitoring; it recommends periodic testicular ultrasound in males with classic CAH, with expert opinion suggesting about every 1-2 years in asymptomatic males. GeneReviews lists testicular adrenal rest tumor monitoring every 3-5 years after puberty. Because intervals differ, local endocrine policy should define the exact interval.
criteria_options.source_section: Long-term surveillance recommendation 6.13 and GeneReviews surveillance
criteria_options.reviewer_input_needed: Local endocrine policy must choose a TART ultrasound interval because Endocrine Society expert opinion and GeneReviews summarize different surveillance intervals.

criteria_options.label: CAH pregnancy/fertility rules: genetic counseling for pregnancy planning, continue hydrocortisone/prednisolone and fludrocortisone, avoid dexamethasone, stress-dose labor
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends genetic counseling for CAH patients/partners planning pregnancy, specialist endocrine management during pregnancy, continuing prepregnancy hydrocortisone or prednisolone and fludrocortisone with adjustment for insufficiency symptoms, considering dose increase in the second or third trimester, stress-dose glucocorticoids during labor and delivery, and avoiding placenta-crossing glucocorticoids such as dexamethasone in pregnancy or conception attempts.
criteria_options.source_section: Genetic counseling, fertility, and pregnancy recommendations 6.3-6.9

criteria_options.label: Prenatal dexamethasone for CAH risk: experimental only, IRB-approved protocol, fetal sex screening, outcomes capture
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society advises prenatal CAH therapy remains experimental, does not recommend specific protocols, and recommends prenatal therapy only through IRB-approved protocols at centers able to collect outcomes, with Y-chromosomal DNA screening in maternal blood to exclude male fetuses from potential treatment groups.
criteria_options.source_section: Prenatal treatment recommendations 2.1-2.3
criteria_options.reviewer_input_needed: Prenatal CAH dexamethasone requires formal research governance, maternal-fetal medicine/endocrinology review, and documented IRB-approved protocol.

criteria_options.label: Crinecerfont adjunct: classic CAH age >=4 years; adults 100 mg twice daily; pediatric doses 25/50/100 mg twice daily by 10-<20, 20-<55, or >=55 kg
criteria_options.criteria_text: DailyMed labeling indicates crinecerfont as adjunctive therapy to glucocorticoid replacement to control androgens in adults and pediatric patients 4 years and older with classic CAH. Adult dose is 100 mg orally twice daily with meals. Pediatric doses are 25 mg twice daily for 10 to less than 20 kg, 50 mg twice daily for 20 to less than 55 kg, and 100 mg twice daily for at least 55 kg. Continue glucocorticoid replacement, assess androstenedione beginning 4 weeks after initiation, and do not reduce glucocorticoid below replacement.
criteria_options.source_section: Indications and dosage sections 1-2

criteria_options.label: Crinecerfont safety: discontinue for clinically significant hypersensitivity, avoid severe renal impairment/ESRD, and double doses for CYP3A4 inducers per label
criteria_options.criteria_text: DailyMed labeling contraindicates crinecerfont in hypersensitivity to crinecerfont or excipients, directs discontinuation for clinically significant hypersensitivity, warns adrenal crisis can occur if glucocorticoids are inadequate, does not recommend use in severe renal impairment or end-stage renal disease, and modifies dosing with CYP3A4 inducers: strong inducers increase morning and evening dosage 2-fold; moderate inducers increase the evening dose 2-fold.
criteria_options.source_section: Contraindications, warnings, renal impairment, and drug interaction sections

criteria_options.label: Virilized 46,XX classic CAH: surgery is optional shared decision in experienced DSD center; consider delaying or observing
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society advises families of pediatric CAH patients, especially minimally virilized girls, be informed about options including delaying surgery or observation; surgery should be performed only in experienced centers with pediatric surgery/urology, endocrinology, anesthesiology, behavioral health, and social work, and decisions for minors remain a family shared decision with experienced consultants.
data_needed: informed consent and assent when applicable
criteria_options.source_section: Restoring functional anatomy by surgery recommendations 7.1-7.4
criteria_options.reviewer_input_needed: Elective genital surgery decisions require experienced DSD team review, family shared decision-making, and local legal/ethical governance.

children.action: Check ABCs, blood pressure/perfusion, temperature, hydration, mental status, glucose, sodium, potassium, CO2/bicarbonate, newborn screen or early morning 17-OHP with assay/timing, cosyntropin steroid profile if borderline, virilization or hyperandrogenism phenotype, growth/bone age or fertility context, current hydrocortisone/prednisone/dexamethasone/fludrocortisone/salt/crinecerfont use, allergies/hypersensitivity, CYP3A4 inducer exposure, renal function, pregnancy/fertility intent, and follow-up reliability.

criteria_options.label: Salt-wasting CAH emergency: infant in first 4 weeks with dehydration, vomiting, shock, low sodium/chloride/CO2, high potassium, hypoglycemia, or inappropriately high urine sodium
criteria_options.criteria_text: GeneReviews identifies any infant with salt-losing crisis in the first 4 weeks of life as a CAH suspect; untreated or poorly controlled salt wasting may show decreased sodium, chloride, and total CO2, increased potassium, and inappropriately high urine sodium.
criteria_options.source_section: GeneReviews suggestive findings and clinical features; Endocrine Society stress/adrenal crisis recommendations

criteria_options.label: CAH stress-dose trigger: febrile illness temperature >38.5 C, dehydration gastroenteritis, major surgery with anesthesia, or major trauma
criteria_options.criteria_text: Increase glucocorticoid dosing in CAH patients who require glucocorticoids for febrile illness >38.5 C, gastroenteritis with dehydration, major surgery with general anesthesia, or major trauma; do not increase for everyday emotional stress or minor illness.
criteria_options.source_section: Stress dosing recommendations 4.7-4.11

criteria_options.label: CAH adrenal crisis injection: hydrocortisone 25 mg infants/preschool, 50 mg school-age children, 100 mg adults; then one-quarter dose every 6 hours
criteria_options.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggested initial parenteral hydrocortisone doses for adrenal crisis are 25 mg for infants and preschool children, 50 mg for school-age children, and 100 mg for adults; successive IV hydrocortisone may be one-quarter of the initial dose every 6 hours.
data_needed: weight if local protocol is weight-based
criteria_options.source_section: Stress dosing Table 4 and adrenal crisis evidence

criteria_options.label: Nonclassic CAH major-stress hydrocortisone only if cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal <14-18 ug/dL (<400-500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic suppression exists
criteria_options.criteria_text: For nonclassic CAH, hydrocortisone stress dosing for major surgery, trauma, or childbirth is suggested only when cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal (<14 to 18 ug/dL or <400 to 500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic adrenal suppression is present.
criteria_options.source_section: Nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia recommendation 5.6

children.action: If blood draw will not delay treatment, send cortisol/ACTH or CAH steroid profile first, then give parenteral hydrocortisone immediately: 25 mg for infants/preschool children, 50 mg for school-age children, or 100 mg for adults. Continue IV hydrocortisone as one-quarter of the initial dose every 6 hours per the CAH guideline, give isotonic saline with dextrose when hypoglycemic or unable to feed, correct potassium/glucose abnormalities, treat infection/trauma/surgical trigger, and admit or monitor at ED/ICU level until perfusion, glucose, sodium, potassium, and oral intake stabilize.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Newborn CAH screen: interpret 17-OHP by gestational age and confirm with LC-MS/MS or cosyntropin profile when first-tier results are positive or borderline
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends 17-hydroxyprogesterone newborn screening norms stratified by gestational age, second-tier LC-MS/MS when available, and cosyntropin stimulation testing before corticosteroids when immunoassay resources cannot improve specificity.
data_needed: cosyntropin plan before steroid treatment when feasible
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Newborn screening recommendations 1.2-1.3 and diagnosis recommendation 3.1

guideline_cutoffs.label: CAH cosyntropin profile: 0.25 mg IV cosyntropin, 0.125 mg in very-low-birth-weight infants, baseline and 60-minute steroids after the first 24-48 hours of life
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: The Endocrine Society diagnostic profile uses 0.25 mg IV cosyntropin, reduced to 0.125 mg in very-low-birth-weight infants, with baseline and 60-minute samples; testing should be deferred until after the first 24 to 48 hours of life because immediate postbirth sampling has false positives and false negatives.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Diagnosis evidence and cosyntropin testing details

guideline_cutoffs.label: After infancy 17-OHP cutoffs: classic CAH >10,000 ng/dL; nonclassic baseline 200-10,000 ng/dL and ACTH-stimulated 1,000-10,000 ng/dL
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: GeneReviews tabulates diagnostic 17-OHP ranges after infancy: classic CAH baseline or ACTH-stimulated 17-OHP greater than 10,000 ng/dL (300 nmol/L); nonclassic CAH baseline 200-10,000 ng/dL (6-300 nmol/L) or ACTH-stimulated 1,000-10,000 ng/dL (31-300 nmol/L); unaffected baseline is below 200 ng/dL and stimulated below 1,000 ng/dL.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: GeneReviews diagnostic testing table; Endocrine Society diagnosis recommendations 3.2-3.4

guideline_cutoffs.label: Salt-wasting CAH emergency: infant in first 4 weeks with dehydration, vomiting, shock, low sodium/chloride/CO2, high potassium, hypoglycemia, or inappropriately high urine sodium
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: GeneReviews identifies any infant with salt-losing crisis in the first 4 weeks of life as a CAH suspect; untreated or poorly controlled salt wasting may show decreased sodium, chloride, and total CO2, increased potassium, and inappropriately high urine sodium.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: GeneReviews suggestive findings and clinical features; Endocrine Society stress/adrenal crisis recommendations

guideline_cutoffs.label: CAH stress-dose trigger: febrile illness temperature >38.5 C, dehydration gastroenteritis, major surgery with anesthesia, or major trauma
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Increase glucocorticoid dosing in CAH patients who require glucocorticoids for febrile illness >38.5 C, gastroenteritis with dehydration, major surgery with general anesthesia, or major trauma; do not increase for everyday emotional stress or minor illness.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Stress dosing recommendations 4.7-4.11

guideline_cutoffs.label: CAH adrenal crisis injection: hydrocortisone 25 mg infants/preschool, 50 mg school-age children, 100 mg adults; then one-quarter dose every 6 hours
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggested initial parenteral hydrocortisone doses for adrenal crisis are 25 mg for infants and preschool children, 50 mg for school-age children, and 100 mg for adults; successive IV hydrocortisone may be one-quarter of the initial dose every 6 hours.
data_needed: weight if local protocol is weight-based
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Stress dosing Table 4 and adrenal crisis evidence

guideline_cutoffs.label: Growing classic CAH maintenance: hydrocortisone 10-15 mg/m2/day three times daily, fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day, and infancy sodium chloride 1-2 g/day
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society Table 2 lists maintenance therapy in growing classic CAH as hydrocortisone tablets 10-15 mg/m2/day divided three times daily, fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day in 1-2 doses, and sodium chloride 1-2 g/day (17-34 mEq/day) in infancy divided into feedings.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Treatment of classic CAH recommendations 4.1-4.6 and Table 2

guideline_cutoffs.label: Adult classic CAH maintenance: hydrocortisone 15-25 mg/day or selected long-acting glucocorticoid plus fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society Table 3 suggests adult classic CAH maintenance options: hydrocortisone 15-25 mg/day in 2-3 doses, prednisone 5-7.5 mg/day in 2 doses, prednisolone 4-6 mg/day in 2 doses, methylprednisolone 4-6 mg/day in 2 doses, dexamethasone 0.25-0.5 mg/day once daily, and fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day in 1-2 doses.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Treatment of classic CAH recommendations 4.4-4.6 and Table 3

guideline_cutoffs.label: Nonclassic CAH major-stress hydrocortisone only if cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal <14-18 ug/dL (<400-500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic suppression exists
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: For nonclassic CAH, hydrocortisone stress dosing for major surgery, trauma, or childbirth is suggested only when cosyntropin cortisol response is suboptimal (<14 to 18 ug/dL or <400 to 500 nmol/L) or iatrogenic adrenal suppression is present.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia recommendation 5.6

guideline_cutoffs.label: Nonclassic CAH daily steroid rules: treat rapid pubarche, advanced bone age, overt virilization, infertility, or patient-important hyperandrogenism; do not treat asymptomatic nonpregnant patients
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society suggests glucocorticoids for children/adolescents with inappropriately early rapid pubarche or bone age advancement, adolescents with overt virilization, and adult women with patient-important hyperandrogenism or infertility; it recommends against glucocorticoids in asymptomatic nonpregnant nonclassic CAH and suggests discontinuation when adult height is attained or symptoms resolve.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Treatment of nonclassic CAH recommendations 5.1-5.5

guideline_cutoffs.label: CAH pediatric monitoring: age <=18 months monitor first 3 months of life and every 3 months thereafter; age >18 months evaluate every 4 months
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: For CAH patients <=18 months, monitor closely in the first 3 months of life and every 3 months thereafter; after 18 months, evaluate every 4 months, with pediatric growth, weight, blood pressure, exam, and biochemical measurements.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Monitoring therapy recommendations 4.12-4.16

guideline_cutoffs.label: CAH monitoring targets: time hormones consistently; normal 17-OHP suggests overtreatment; follicular progesterone target <0.6 ng/mL when trying to conceive
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society advises consistently timed hormone measurements relative to medication and time of day, not fully suppressing endogenous adrenal steroids, and interpreting 17-OHP normalization as overtreatment; for women trying to conceive, follicular-phase progesterone target is below 0.6 ng/mL (2 nmol/L).
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Monitoring therapy evidence and Table 5

guideline_cutoffs.label: CAH long-term surveillance: adults need annual exam; TART ultrasound interval differs by source and should use local endocrine policy
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends annual adult physical examination with blood pressure, BMI, Cushingoid features, and biochemical monitoring; it recommends periodic testicular ultrasound in males with classic CAH, with expert opinion suggesting about every 1-2 years in asymptomatic males. GeneReviews lists testicular adrenal rest tumor monitoring every 3-5 years after puberty. Because intervals differ, local endocrine policy should define the exact interval.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Long-term surveillance recommendation 6.13 and GeneReviews surveillance
guideline_cutoffs.reviewer_input_needed: Local endocrine policy must choose a TART ultrasound interval because Endocrine Society expert opinion and GeneReviews summarize different surveillance intervals.

guideline_cutoffs.label: CAH pregnancy/fertility rules: genetic counseling for pregnancy planning, continue hydrocortisone/prednisolone and fludrocortisone, avoid dexamethasone, stress-dose labor
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society recommends genetic counseling for CAH patients/partners planning pregnancy, specialist endocrine management during pregnancy, continuing prepregnancy hydrocortisone or prednisolone and fludrocortisone with adjustment for insufficiency symptoms, considering dose increase in the second or third trimester, stress-dose glucocorticoids during labor and delivery, and avoiding placenta-crossing glucocorticoids such as dexamethasone in pregnancy or conception attempts.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Genetic counseling, fertility, and pregnancy recommendations 6.3-6.9

guideline_cutoffs.label: Prenatal dexamethasone for CAH risk: experimental only, IRB-approved protocol, fetal sex screening, outcomes capture
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society advises prenatal CAH therapy remains experimental, does not recommend specific protocols, and recommends prenatal therapy only through IRB-approved protocols at centers able to collect outcomes, with Y-chromosomal DNA screening in maternal blood to exclude male fetuses from potential treatment groups.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Prenatal treatment recommendations 2.1-2.3
guideline_cutoffs.reviewer_input_needed: Prenatal CAH dexamethasone requires formal research governance, maternal-fetal medicine/endocrinology review, and documented IRB-approved protocol.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Crinecerfont adjunct: classic CAH age >=4 years; adults 100 mg twice daily; pediatric doses 25/50/100 mg twice daily by 10-<20, 20-<55, or >=55 kg
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: DailyMed labeling indicates crinecerfont as adjunctive therapy to glucocorticoid replacement to control androgens in adults and pediatric patients 4 years and older with classic CAH. Adult dose is 100 mg orally twice daily with meals. Pediatric doses are 25 mg twice daily for 10 to less than 20 kg, 50 mg twice daily for 20 to less than 55 kg, and 100 mg twice daily for at least 55 kg. Continue glucocorticoid replacement, assess androstenedione beginning 4 weeks after initiation, and do not reduce glucocorticoid below replacement.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Indications and dosage sections 1-2

guideline_cutoffs.label: Crinecerfont safety: discontinue for clinically significant hypersensitivity, avoid severe renal impairment/ESRD, and double doses for CYP3A4 inducers per label
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: DailyMed labeling contraindicates crinecerfont in hypersensitivity to crinecerfont or excipients, directs discontinuation for clinically significant hypersensitivity, warns adrenal crisis can occur if glucocorticoids are inadequate, does not recommend use in severe renal impairment or end-stage renal disease, and modifies dosing with CYP3A4 inducers: strong inducers increase morning and evening dosage 2-fold; moderate inducers increase the evening dose 2-fold.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Contraindications, warnings, renal impairment, and drug interaction sections

guideline_cutoffs.label: Virilized 46,XX classic CAH: surgery is optional shared decision in experienced DSD center; consider delaying or observing
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Endocrine Society advises families of pediatric CAH patients, especially minimally virilized girls, be informed about options including delaying surgery or observation; surgery should be performed only in experienced centers with pediatric surgery/urology, endocrinology, anesthesiology, behavioral health, and social work, and decisions for minors remain a family shared decision with experienced consultants.
data_needed: informed consent and assent when applicable
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Restoring functional anatomy by surgery recommendations 7.1-7.4
guideline_cutoffs.reviewer_input_needed: Elective genital surgery decisions require experienced DSD team review, family shared decision-making, and local legal/ethical governance.

children.label: Newborn/classic CAH confirmation
children.edgeLabel: Newborn branch: positive screen, ambiguous genitalia/virilization, poor feeding, vomiting, weight loss, dehydration, or family history with age/gestation-adjusted 17-OHP pending

criteria.description: Use for stable newborns or infants with a positive CAH screen, virilization, family risk, or early salt-wasting concern who are not already in adrenal crisis.

children.action: Refer to pediatric endocrinology when regionally available, check serum 17-OHP with gestational-age/birth-weight interpretation, sodium, potassium, glucose, CO2/bicarbonate, renin/aldosterone when salt wasting is possible, and a second-tier LC-MS/MS or cosyntropin steroid profile when first-tier testing is positive or borderline. Do not use immediate postbirth cosyntropin sampling before 24-48 hours unless endocrine review determines benefit outweighs false result risk.
